1: Then all the tribes of Israel came to David at Hebron and said: We are your bone and flesh. The phrase ʿaṣməḵā ûḇəśārəḵā (your bone and your flesh) is the covenant kinship formula first used by Laban to Jacob (Gen 29:14) and later by Abimelech appealing to Shechem (Judg 9:2). It signifies genuine genealogical and covenant kinship — the northern tribes are asserting that the political division after Saul's death was never a repudiation of their bond with David but a temporary alignment with Ish-bosheth that has now ended.
2: Even when Saul was our king, it was you who led Israel out and brought it back. And YHWH said to you: You shall shepherd (tirʿeh) my people Israel, and you shall be prince (nāgîd) over Israel. The dual vocabulary — rōʿeh (shepherd) and nāgîd (designated leader, not the more dynastic melek) — is significant. The shepherd image comes from David's background (1 Sam 16-17) and from ANE royal ideology where the king is the shepherd of his people (Hammurabi's code prologue, Assyrian royal inscriptions). nāgîd (rather than melek) emphasizes the appointment comes from YHWH, not from popular acclamation or dynastic succession.
3: So all the elders of Israel came to King David at Hebron, and King David made a covenant with them at Hebron before YHWH, and they anointed David king over Israel. David's third anointing: first by Samuel (1 Sam 16:13), second by the men of Judah at Hebron (2:4), now by all Israel at Hebron. Each anointing marks a stage of the Davidic kingship's expansion. The covenant David makes with the elders (wayyikrōṯ lāhem dāwid bərîṯ) is the formal political compact of the constitutional monarchy — the king has obligations to the people as well as authority over them.
4: David was thirty years old when he became king (ben-šəlōšîm šānāh dāwid bəmālkô), and he reigned forty years. The note of age and total reign is the regnal formula used throughout Kings — standardized biographical data for each monarch. David's age thirty is significant: the same age at which Levites began their tabernacle service (Num 4:3) and at which Joseph entered Pharaoh's service (Gen 41:46). Later tradition noted that Jesus began his ministry at about thirty (Luke 3:23), likely in conscious resonance with these precedents.
5: At Hebron he reigned over Judah seven years and six months, and at Jerusalem he reigned over all Israel and Judah thirty-three years. The two-capital structure — seven-and-a-half years at Hebron over Judah, then thirty-three years at Jerusalem over all Israel — frames the total of forty years. Jerusalem becomes the capital that unifies the northern and southern tribal blocks precisely because it was not previously associated with either grouping.
6: The king and his men went to Jerusalem against the Jebusites, the inhabitants of the land, who said to David: You will not come in here, but the blind and lame will turn you back — thinking: David cannot come in here. Jerusalem had remained unconquered through the entire period of the judges (Judg 1:21: the tribe of Benjamin did not drive out the Jebusites). The Jebusite taunt about 'the blind and the lame' defending the city is interpreted in v8 — it may reference the Jebusite belief that their disabled would be sufficient to defend them, or possibly that images/statues were placed on the walls. The strategic value of Jerusalem as a neutral site on the tribal border of Benjamin and Judah was exactly why David chose it.
7: Nevertheless, David took the stronghold of Zion — that is, the City of David. Zion (ṣiyyôn) appears here for the first time as the name for the Jebusite stronghold. Its etymology is uncertain; it becomes the primary theological name for Jerusalem in the Psalms and prophets. The 'City of David' designation begins here — the southeastern ridge of the Ophel, the oldest part of the city, which has been the focus of archaeological excavations from Kathleen Kenyon (1961-67) through Eilat Mazar and Yosef Garfinkel in more recent decades.
8: David said on that day: Whoever strikes the Jebusites, let him use the water shaft (ṣinnôr)... The water shaft (ṣinnôr) is connected by many archaeologists to Warren's Shaft — the vertical water system discovered by Charles Warren in 1867, providing subterranean access from inside the city to the Gihon Spring below. If soldiers could ascend through this system, the city's water-supply access became its point of vulnerability. 1 Chr 11:6 identifies Joab as the one who went up first through this route, explaining his appointment as commander. The reference to the blind and lame in v8 establishes a royal decree excluding them from the temple (though the tradition became complex in NT period).
9: David lived in the stronghold and called it the City of David. And David built the city all around from the Millo inward. The Millōʾ (from mālēʾ, to fill) refers to a fill structure — a terraced earthwork platform that extended the usable area of the narrow ridge. The Stepped Stone Structure discovered by Kenyon and further excavated by Eilat Mazar is the most likely candidate for the Millo, supporting a large building on the upper part of the City of David ridge. The phrase 'from the Millo inward' suggests the Millo marked the northern boundary of the original city expansion.
10: And David became greater and greater, for YHWH, the God of hosts, was with him (wayyēleḵ dāwid hālôk wəgāḏôl waYHWH ʾelōhê ṣəḇāʾôṯ ʿimmô). The formula — progressive growth + divine presence — is the standard narrative indicator that a biblical figure is operating under covenant blessing. YHWH ṣəḇāʾôṯ (God of hosts/armies) is the name associated with the ark (1 Sam 4:4) and with YHWH's military leadership — its use here reinforces the connection between David's military success and the ark's arrival in Jerusalem in ch6.
11: Hiram king of Tyre sent messengers to David with cedar trees, and carpenters and stonemasons, and they built David a house. Hiram I of Tyre (reigned c. 969-936 BCE by the higher chronology, or approximately contemporary with David) is the first Phoenician king to appear in the biblical narrative. The cedar trade from the Lebanese mountains was the primary luxury construction material in the ancient Levant — cedar was scarce in Canaan and exported by Phoenician maritime merchants. Phoenician craftsmen were specialists in ashlar stone construction; their appearance in David's palace marks the beginning of the Phoenician-Israelite architectural partnership that will culminate in Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 5-7).
12: And David knew that YHWH had established him as king over Israel, and that he had exalted his kingdom for the sake of his people Israel. David's theological interpretation of his own success is the interpretive lens the narrative provides: the consolidation of the kingdom, the building of the palace, the Phoenician alliance — all are read as YHWH's action on behalf of his people, not David's personal achievement. The phrase lǝmaʿan ʿammô yiśrāʾēl (for the sake of his people Israel) places Israel as the ultimate beneficiary of the Davidic kingship.
13: And David took more concubines and wives from Jerusalem, after he came from Hebron, and more sons and daughters were born to David. The multiplication of wives in Jerusalem is the ANE practice of royal polygamy — kings used marriage alliances to seal political relationships. Deut 17:17 warned the future king not to multiply wives (lō yarbeh-lô nāšîm), a prohibition that David begins to violate here. The sons born in Jerusalem become the succession problem that drives the narrative from ch13 onward.
14: And these are the names of those who were born to him in Jerusalem: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon, Ibhar, Elishua, Nepheg, Japhia, Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet. The Jerusalem-born sons are distinguished from the sons born at Hebron (3:2-5). Solomon appears fourth in this list — not the eldest son, which will become a succession problem requiring divine designation and human maneuvering (1 Kgs 1-2). The name Nathan appears here — a son of David named after the prophet, though this Nathan plays no role in the narrative.
15: Ibhar, Elishua, Nepheg, Japhia... (continuation of the list). The census of sons signals the expansion of the royal house. In ANE terms, a large royal family was evidence of divine blessing and political stability. The sons born to David in Jerusalem represent both the fulfillment of covenant blessing and the seeds of the succession crisis that will dominate 1 Kings.
16: Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet. The list ends with eleven names in this account (1 Chr 3:5-8 lists sixteen sons of the Jerusalem wives including four by Bathsheba). The difference may reflect different sources or different periods of compilation. The list's function is genealogical record-keeping that the later succession narratives will draw on.
17: When the Philistines heard that they had anointed David king over all Israel, all the Philistines went up to search for David. But David heard of it and went down to the stronghold. The Philistines had tolerated David as a vassal king over Judah (2:1-4) but the unification of all Israel under David represents a strategic threat they could not ignore. The 'stronghold' (məṣûḏāh) to which David descends may be the Cave of Adullam (1 Sam 22:1) or another Judean fortress. The Philistine campaign gives David the opportunity to consolidate his military position before his Jerusalem capital is fully secured.
18: Now the Philistines had come and spread out in the Valley of Rephaim (ʿēmeq rəpāʾîm). The Valley of Rephaim (modern Baqa/Baq'a, southwest of Jerusalem) was a fertile plain connecting the Shephelah to Jerusalem — a natural invasion corridor. The name connects to the Rephaim, the pre-Israelite inhabitants described elsewhere as giants. The valley's agricultural value (it was a grain-growing area, Isa 17:5) and its strategic position made it a recurring battleground.
19: And David inquired of YHWH, Shall I go up against the Philistines? Will you give them into my hand? And YHWH said to David, Go up, for I will certainly give the Philistines into your hand. The inquiry of YHWH before battle — presumably through the ephod and Urim/Thummim — is the practice the earlier narrative established (1 Sam 23:2,4; 30:8). David's consistent practice of seeking divine direction contrasts with Saul's unauthorized sacrifice (1 Sam 13) and resort to necromancy (1 Sam 28). The divine guarantee (kî nāṯōn ʾettēn, I will surely give) uses the infinitive-absolute emphasis.
20: And David came to Baal-perazim, and David defeated them there. And he said, YHWH has burst through my enemies before me like a bursting flood of waters. Therefore the name of that place is called Baal-perazim. The place-name Baal-perazim (lord/owner of breaking-through) is a commemorative name David coins on the spot — a practice common in the OT for theophanic or battle sites. The image of YHWH as a flood bursting through (*pāraṣ*) anticipates prophetic imagery of YHWH's irresistible advance (Isa 28:17, 30:28).
21: And they abandoned their idols there, and David and his men carried them off. The abandoned Philistine idols reflect the ANE practice of armies carrying their national deities into battle — the god's presence was thought to ensure victory. Their abandonment in retreat is the inversion of the ark narrative: where the Philistines captured Israel's ark (1 Sam 4-5), Israel now captures Philistia's idols. 1 Chr 14:12 adds that David commanded the idols to be burned — stricter compliance with Deut 7:5,25.
22: And the Philistines came up yet again and spread out in the Valley of Rephaim. A second invasion, in the same location — the Philistines have regrouped and returned. David inquires of YHWH again (v23) rather than assuming the previous oracle covers the new situation. The two-battle structure (vv17-21 and vv22-25) demonstrates that each campaign requires fresh divine guidance, not a standing permission to attack.
23: And David inquired of YHWH, and he said, You shall not go up; go around to their rear, and come against them opposite the balsam trees (bəkāʾîm). The different strategy for the second battle — flanking movement rather than frontal assault — indicates YHWH's tactical direction is specific to each situation. The bəkāʾîm (balsam trees, or possibly Aspen/mulberry) marked the terrain feature for the flanking position. The balsam tree has not been definitively identified archaeologically.
24: And when you hear the sound of marching (qôl ṣaʿăḏāh) in the tops of the balsam trees, then act, for then YHWH has gone out before you to strike the army of the Philistines. The signal is a natural phenomenon — the sound of wind moving through the treetops — interpreted as YHWH's advance. This is an example of YHWH using natural events as the timing signal for military action. The theophanic 'going out' of YHWH as a warrior before Israel is the imagery of the old Holy War tradition (Judg 4:14; 5:4-5).
25: And David did as YHWH commanded him, and struck down the Philistines from Geba to Gezer. The geographic spread — Geba (in Benjamin, northeast of Jerusalem) to Gezer (in the Shephelah, northwest) — indicates a decisive sweep that drove the Philistines back to their coastal territory. The two battles of the Valley of Rephaim effectively ended Philistine control of the highlands and secured Jerusalem as the Israelite capital free from immediate military threat.
